coredata.dc:description: In the present study, the thermal decomposition of citric acid in the presence of biogenic amine was used to synthesize four different functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs), namely, histamine-(HCQDs), putrescine-(PCQDs), cadaverine-(CCQDs) and spermine-(SCQDs). The thermal decomposition of the precursors resulted in a decrease in stability and the formation of surface amides via a cross-linking process between the carboxyl and amine groups. The deposition of biogenic amines was confirmed by a structural characterization of the synthesized CQDs. The resulting CQDs, with a net zero charge, exhibited excellent stability in environments with different pH values. Through a set of different cytotoxicity tests, the absence of gene mutations, apoptosis, necrosis or disruption in cell membranes revealed the high biocompatibility of the CQDs. The antimicrobial activity of the synthesized CQDs was investigated against different bacterial species (Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Klebsiella pneumonia). We determined the growth kinetics, production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), cell viability and changes in membrane integrity by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The minimal inhibitory concentrations (MICs) for S. aureus ranged from 3.4 to 6.9 µg/mL. Regarding E.coli and K. pneumonia, all CQD formulations reduced growth, and the MICs were determined for CCQDs and HCQDs (6.9–19.4 µg/mL). The antibacterial activity mechanism was attributed to the oxidative stress generated after CQD treatment, which resulted in the destabilization of the bacterial membrane. The bacterial permeability to propidium iodide indicated a change in membrane integrity, and the effect of CQDs on the morphology of the bacterial cells was evidenced by SEM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM characterization of the CQDs modified with natural amines. (A) cadaverine – CCQDs, (B) histamine – HCQDs, (C) putrescine – PCQDs, (D) spermine – SCQDs, and (E) bare CQDs.

Amine-modified CQD size and surface charge obtained by dynamic light scattering measurements using a detector angle of 173°, wavelength of 633 nm, refractive index of 0.30, and real refractive index of 1.33. The size and zeta potential of CQDs modified with A) cadaverine – CCQDs, B) histamine – HCQDs, C) putrescine – PCQDs, D) spermine – SCQDs, and E) CQDs without amine modification. All measurements were performed in triplicate (mean ± SD for n = 3). The trend of peaks was the same for each measured sample.

Characterization of the amine-modified CQDs using spectroscopy methods. (A) Different baseline-corrected FTIR spectra of CCQDs, HCQDs, PCQDs and HCQDs in the range 4000 to 400 cm−1. C 1s and N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of CQDs modified with natural amines: (B) cadaverine – CCQDs, (C) histamine – HCQDs, (D) putrescine – PCQDs, and (E) spermine SCQDs. All measurements were performed in triplicate. The trend of peaks was the same for each measured sample (n = 3).

Thermogravimetric analysis and X-ray diffraction of CQDs modified with natural amines. (A) Decomposition curves of CQDs modified with cadavarine (CCQDs), histamine (HCQDs), putrescine (PCQDs) and spermine (SCQDs) heated at 10 °C/min to 500 °C in N2; (B) decomposition curves of the materials used for CQD synthesis and coating heated at 10 °C/min to 500 °C in N2 where CA stands for citric acid, DETA – diethylenetriamine, CAD – cadavarine, HIS – histamine, PUT – putrescine and SPE – spermine; and (C) XRD pattern of CQDs modified with natural amines (cadaverine – CCQDs; histamine – HCQDs; putrescine – PCQDs; and spermine – SCQDs) with broad diffraction peaks observed and centered at 2θ = 23°.

Spectrophotometry analysis of CQDs modified with natural amines. (A) UV–Vis emission spectra of the materials used for the CQD synthesis and coating scanned from250 and 300to850 nm; (B) UV–Vis emission spectra of the CQDs modified with cadaverine (CCQDs), histamine (HCQDs), putrescine (PCQDs) and spermine (SCQDs) scanned from 250 and 300 to 850 nm; and (C) fluorescence dependence of CQDs modified with natural amine over time (0–5 weeks) in Milli-Q H2O, neutral cellular simulant fluid (human plasma environment) and acidic cellular simulant fluid (acidic intracellular environment). The stability was studied at 25 °C without the presence of light. All measurements were performed in triplicate (mean ± SD for n = 3). The trend of peaks was the same for each measured sample.

(A) MTT assay results of the amine-modified CQD cytotoxic effect on HaCaT and HEK-293 cell lines observed after 24 h in a concentration range of 0–500 µg/mL and (B) internalization effect of CQDs modified with cadavarine (CCQDs), histamine (HCQDs), putrescine (PCQDs) and spermine (SCQDs) on the morphology of HaCaT and HEK-293 cell lines investigated at the highest applied concentration of 500 μg/mL (lyophilized CQDs) by phase-contrast microscopy. All measurements were performed in triplicate (mean ± SD for n = 3).

Representative confocal micrographs of HaCaT and HEK-293 cell lines treated with amine-modified CQDs for 24 h, which were successively fixed and visualized for the F-actin cytoskeletal network and CQDs. The scale bar in the figure is 20 μm. The scale bar in the 20x-magnified pictures is 1 μm.

Living cell fluorescence imaging showing CQD nuclear localization using DAPI staining and the formation of ROS (CellRox) in (A) HaCaT and (B) HEK-293 cell lines observed for 24 h at the highest applied concentration of 500 μg/mL (lyophilized CQDs).

(A) DNA damage in the HaCaT and HEK-293 cell lines as investigated by the comet assay after treatment with the highest CQDs concentration of 500 μg/mL (lyophilized CQDs) and observed after 24 h using a fluorescence microscope; (B) comet grades of the HaCaT and HEK-293 cell line DNA compared to PBS (negative control – pH7.4) and hydrogen peroxide (positive control – 150 μM H2O2) presented as the mean ± SD for n = 3. Propidium iodide was used for the observation of DNA.

Antibacterial testing of various types of modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Inhibition curves of antibacterial activity of (A) spermine-CQDs, (B) putrescine-CQDs, (C) cadaverine-CQDs, and (D) histamine-CQDs on S. aureus, E. coli and K. pneumoniae. All measurements were performed in triplicate (mean ± SD for n = 3). Fluorescence microscopy was conducted for live (SYTO 9, green) and dead cells (PI, red) with the (E) spermine-CQDs and (F) putrescine-CQDs on S. aureus; the scale bar is 20 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effect of amine-modified CQDs on ROS formation and the morphology of S. aureus. (A) Fluorescence microscopy for the detection of ROS using CellROX (green reagent, green) and Hoechst 33,342 (blue) for the imaging of all S. aureus cells after treatment with putrescine-CQDs; the scale bar is 20 µm. Cryo-SEM images of the antibacterial effect of (B) spermine-CQDs and (C) putrescine-CQDs on S. aureus; the scale bar is 1 µm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Decomposition steps of the QDs heated in nitrogen at 2 and 10 °C/min(numbers 2 and 10 in CQDs): weight loss in each step (Δwn, %), temperature of the fastest weight loss in each step (Td, °C). The TGA analysis for each specimen was performed as the mean ± SD for n = 2.
Calculation of the amine concentrations within the carbon core using CHNS elemental analysis. All measurements were performed in triplicate (mean ± SD for n = 3).
MICs (µg/mL) determined from the growth curves. All measurements were performed in triplicate (mean ± SD for n = 3).

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of natural amine-modified biocompatible carbon quantum dots with antibacterial activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, the thermal decomposition of citric acid in the presence of biogenic amine was used to synthesize four different functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs), namely, histamine-(HCQDs), putrescine-(PCQDs), cadaverine-(CCQDs) and spermine-(SCQDs). The thermal decomposition of the precursors resulted in a decrease in stability and the formation of surface amides via a cross-linking process between the carboxyl and amine groups. The deposition of biogenic amines was confirmed by a structural characterization of the synthesized CQDs. The resulting CQDs, with a net zero charge, exhibited excellent stability in environments with different pH values. Through a set of different cytotoxicity tests, the absence of gene mutations, apoptosis, necrosis or disruption in cell membranes revealed the high biocompatibility of the CQDs. The antimicrobial activity of the synthesized CQDs was investigated against different bacterial species (Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Klebsiella pneumonia). We determined the growth kinetics, production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), cell viability and changes in membrane integrity by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The minimal inhibitory concentrations (MICs) for S. aureus ranged from 3.4 to 6.9 µg/mL. Regarding E.coli and K. pneumonia, all CQD formulations reduced growth, and the MICs were determined for CCQDs and HCQDs (6.9–19.4 µg/mL). The antibacterial activity mechanism was attributed to the oxidative stress generated after CQD treatment, which resulted in the destabilization of the bacterial membrane. The bacterial permeability to propidium iodide indicated a change in membrane integrity, and the effect of CQDs on the morphology of the bacterial cells was evidenced by SEM.

ce:section.ce:para: Advances in nanotechnology and the precise control over nanoscale systems have encouraged various nanoparticle applications, such as in drug delivery, tissue engineering, and imaging and as therapeutic agents. Altering the physical and biological properties of nanoparticles allow their performance to be tailored toward the generation of biocompatible nanomedicines with prolonged circulation, reduced immunogenicity, enhanced safety and improved solubilization. The antibacterial properties of nanoparticles are ascribed to their ability to penetrate bacterial cells and interfere with important molecular pathways, thus offering unique strategies to combat the emerging threat of bacterial resistance [1]. There are different types of nanoparticles employed for antibacterial application, and those made with Ag, Au, Cu or Fe are shown to be the most effective. However, despite their high antibacterial potential, their clinical use remains challenging. Furthermore, considerable evidence suggests their potentially toxic effect on mammalian cells, which cannot be ignored. The nanoparticle size and surface modifications are cited as the major sources of toxicity. Smaller nanoparticles have the ability to enter cells without limitation, causing serious cellular damage (violate the cell membrane ion exchange system, catalyze oxidative damage, trigger lipid peroxidation or release toxic ions). On the other hand, the nanoparticle size and surface modifications are responsible for the available surface area and charge. A large surface area and surface charge directly contribute to the toxicity of nanoparticles through various mechanisms, namely, triggering protein aggregation, damaging DNA or disrupting membrane activity [2–4]. While the exact mechanisms for the various toxic complications are unclear, researchers investigating these issues have reported that coupling nanoparticles with biomolecules/natural compounds may endow the nanoparticles with the desired size along with being biodegradable, biocompatible, and nontoxic.

Natural amines are a group of low-molecular-mass nitrogenous compounds mainly formed through the decarboxylation of corresponding amino acids. Putrescine, spermine, cadaverine and histamine are produced in cells and are essential for cellular functions, such as membrane stabilization, immune response, growth and cell signaling [5]. The above amines have a highly charged nature and an ability to interact with cellular macromolecules potentially making them very promising candidates for biomedical applications [6–8]. In addition, if these amines are assembled into nanoparticles, benefits, such as heat tolerability and solubility and the possibility of using them as surfactants in a well-controlled manner, may help mitigate the concerns associated with commonly used nanoparticles [9].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) represent a novel class of fluorescent metal-free nanoparticles consisting of a carbon core surrounded by amorphous carbon frames. CQDs have attracted attention due to their advantageous intrinsic properties, such as low toxicity, photostability, high water dispersibility, good biocompatibility and simple and inexpensive synthesis routes [10,11]. Different approaches have been used in the synthesis of CQDs, including (i) top-down routes (breaking large carbon materials), such as laser ablation, electrochemical exfoliation and chemical oxidation, or (ii) bottom-up routes (building from smaller precursors), such as microwave synthesis and plasma, hydrothermal and solvothermal treatments [12–14]. The antibacterial activity and low toxicity of CQDs are reported to be dependent on their size and surface charge, which can be easily tuned through surface functionalization (e.g., carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, epoxy, amides, etc.) [15]. Membrane disruption and the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) are important mechanisms in mediating CQD-induced bactericidal activity [16,17]. The adsorption of CQDs to the bacterial membrane induces changes in the membrane permeability, leading to cytoplasm leakage [15,18]. Increased ROS levels and oxidative stress alter the structure of DNA and proteins [19,20]. Although effective, CQDs are rarely used in conjunction with other carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene oxide, single-walled carbon nanotubes and fullerene [21]. The antibacterial properties of these carbon-based nanomaterials have been extensively studied, and similar systematic investigations toward understanding the interdependence of CQD surface chemistry and bactericidal performance are necessary [22]. A major obstacle to the final application of CQDs is the lack of detailed knowledge about their biological effects. Within the biological environment, they are prone to interact with biomolecules, leading to the formation of biomolecular coronas and their rapid clearance from the bloodstream. A strategy to combat undesired adsorption may be the fabrication of charge neutral CQDs. Therefore, we postulated that the carbonization of citric acid and biogenic amine molecules with charged amino and carboxyl groups will result in CQDs that are neutral overall and have the desired nonadhesive properties.

Herein, we report a one-pot synthesis of diversely functionalized CQDs by the thermal decomposition route using citric acid (CA) as the source of carbon and diethylenetriamine (DETA) for the surface modification in the first step. In the second step, the synthesized CQDs were capped with biogenic amines, including histamine (HCQDs), putrescine (PCQDs), cadaverine (CCQDs) and spermine (SCQDs), under microwave irradiation without a coupling agent. The fundamental criteria that directed the synthesis of our CQDs were (1) easy functionalization, (2) stability over a large pH range, (3) low nonspecific adsorption, and (4) a neutral charge and small size essential for accessing confined biological compartments. Using CA and biogenic amines, beneficial CQDs that met all these criteria were prepared. Their successful synthesis and functionalization have been confirmed via various characterization methods, such as UV–Vis spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), atomic force microscopy (AFM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). These studies reveal that the natural amines are efficiently conjugated to form functionalized CQDs. A key advantage of our synthesized CQDs is their zero net charge. The neutral charge should result in high colloidal stability regardless of the pH, thus exhibiting nontoxic and noninteracting behavior. Furthermore, the neutral charge will diminish the possibility of ionic interactions with the charged components of the bacterial membrane. It is expected that the functional groups on the CQD surface will influence the antibacterial properties without the surface charge effect. Therefore, the potential of these newly synthesized CQDs to be used in antibacterial applications was explored. The antibacterial properties of amine CQDs were tested against different gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) and gram-negative (Escherichia coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae) species showing efficient antibacterial properties likely associated with the generation of ROS. Once the CQDs were inside, they induced the generation of ROS and triggered cell death due to irreparable DNA damage. Alterations in the cell morphology of the CQD-treated bacterial cells were observed by cryo-SEM, showing significant changes in the structure of the S. aureus cell wall. Cryo-SEM revealed that after the CQD treatment, the membrane surface was covered with the visible formation of a crystal structure, which probably belonged to the ion aggregation triggered by the uncontrolled influx/efflux of ions across the membrane. Furthermore, the toxicity toward human cell lines was evaluated. The high biocompatibility of amine-modified CQDs was reflected in their negligible cytotoxicity against HaCaT and HEK-293 cells. Our findings indicate that the design of neutrally charged CQDs may have a promising future as novel antibacterial nanoparticles.

The chemicals and solvents used for the synthesis and characterization of CQDs were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA) in ACS purity and were used without further purification. Milli-Q water was obtained by Ultrapure Lab Water Systems (Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany).

The synthesis of water soluble CQDs was performed in 4-mL screw top vials using a Multiwave 3000 Microwave Reaction System equipped with a 64MG5 rotor (Anton Paar Multiwave3000, Prague, Czech Republic). Briefly, CQDs were synthetized using 1 g of CA dissolved in 4 mL of Milli-Q water under magnetic stirring (IKA C-MAG HS-7, IKA®-Werke GmbH & Co. KG, Germany), followed by the addition of 0.5 mL of DETA and 100 μg or0.1 mL of the respective amine (histamine, cadaverine, putrescine dihydrochloride or spermine tetrahydrochloride) to obtain a total volume of 5 mL. The synthesis was performed by a microwave reaction system at 130 °C, with a heating time of 20 min and a microwave power of 300 W. This amine modified CQD synthesis was repeated for every amine used for surface functionalization. Synthesized CQDs were left for 10 min to cool at room temperature. To remove unreacted chemicals, the synthesized amine CQD solutions were dialyzed for 72 h using d-Tube Maxi (3 KDa or 1.5 nm filters according to producer specification) dialyzers with the Milli-Q water changed every 2 h. d-Tube Maxi dialyzers were supplied by Millipore (EMD, San Diego, CA, USA). Before further applications, the CQDs were lyophilized by Christ Alpha (B. Braun Biotech, Basel, Switzerland) for 24 h at −70 °C and 0.0026 mBar. All further investigations were performed after dissolving the lyophilized CQDs in Milli-Q water.
The method and materials used in the characterization of the amine-modified CQDs and studies of the amine-modified CQD biological activity are described in detail in the Supporting Information.

CQD properties depend on the carbon source used for the preparation and on the surface modification achieved by doping different molecules. If the CQD surface is decorated to mimic biological surfaces, there is a high possibility that a biocompatible nanomaterial is generated. This approach has been used in several applications, including bioimaging, cellular tracking and medical treatment for a wide range of bacterial species [23]. The reported one-pot synthesis of different amino-based functionalized CQDs is based on the decomposition of CA and the polymerization of carboxyl groups (CA) and amine groups (from amines), thus resulting in amide formation on the surface of the newly synthesized CQDs. Further exposure of the polymerized CQDs to constant heat at high temperature leads to the carbonization and formation of CQDs with the desired neutral charge [24,25].

The newly synthesized CQDs modified with different amines were analyzed and characterized after removing all impurities by dialyzation and lyophilization. The morphological study of amine modified CQDs was performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) clearly showed that particles are formed successful (Fig. 1 (A–E)). The CQDs were found to be polydisperse and spherical, with the average size in the range 6 ± 2 nm. The TEM images showed the partially presence of the formed chain-like aggregates, which mostly arise from the draining of samples before the measurements. The successful formation of CQDs was also confirmed by AFM (see Fig. S1). From the AFM micrographs, the synthesized amine-modified CQDs show a narrow size distribution in the range of 3 to 8 nm with an average diameter of approximately 3.0 nm in the case of CCQDs, 4.6 nm for HCQDs, 4.0 nm for PCQDs and 8.0 nm for SCQDs (Fig. S1(A–D)). The amine CQDs were found to be spherical and well distributed. However, by the segmentation method, we identified a certain number of particles in clusters, thus indicating the small tendency of particles to aggregate (see Fig. S2). Furthermore, the AFM 3D scan profile revealed that the formed CQDs had a multilayered structure with an average CQD thickness of 2 nm, which clearly confirmed the presence of nanoparticles (Fig. S1(A–D)). It has been reported that most nitrogen-doped CQDs are multilayered due to the formation of a C–N layer on the surface, which can be arranged in several layers during synthesis [26,27].

To verify the data obtained by AFM, the size distribution profiles of amine CQDs were investigated using DLS (Fig. 2 ). The original DLS graph and zeta potential data can be found in the Supporting Information (see Fig. S3). The DLS determined average particle sizes for all of the synthesized amine-modified CQDs were found to bein the narrow range of 4 ± 2 nm (Fig. 2(A–D)). An additional peak, approximately 8 nm, was detected only in the case of SCQDs and corresponded to the results obtained by AFM. Interestingly, for the others (PCQDs, HCQDs, and CCQDs) analyzed by DLS, additional peaks were not detected, indicating a good distribution of monodispersed nanoparticles. However, such results are in contradiction with the AFM segmentation method. Therefore, the aggregation of amine CQDs was probably due to the sample drying process, which was necessary prior to AFM analysis [28]. Furthermore, the formation of the CQD multilayered structure was confirmed by following the differences in particle diameters between the amine-modified and unmodified CQDs. DLS confirmed that the size of CQDs without modification was approximately2 ± 1 nm with good dispersity and a narrow size distribution (see Fig. 2(E)). Differences in size between the modified and unmodified CQDs strongly suggested that amines were deposited (C–N layers) on the surface of the formed CQDs after synthesis.

The surface charges of the synthesized CQDs were studied in terms of ζ-potential measurements. We observed a significant shift in the surface charge between the bare CQDs and amine-modified CQDs (see Fig. S3(A–E)). The ζ-potential of bare CQDs was +15.6 mV in comparison with the ζ-potentials of CCQDs, HCQDs, PCQDs and SCQDs, which ranged from 0 to 2 mV. All measurements were performed in triplicate. The trend of peaks was the same for each measured sample. The shift of the positive potential toward neutral with a slightly positive ζ-potential was the result of the amine/acid ratio used for the synthesis of CCQDs, HCQDs, PCQDs and SCQDs. Karakocak et al. reported that using amine/acid ratios of 0.2–0.8:1 generated CQDs with neutral and slightly positive ζ-potentials [29]. They also revealed that different buffers or solvents did not have an effect on the surface charge of particles, confirming that the amine/acid ratio primarily influenced the CQD charge. Therefore, the neutral charge of amine CQDs probably caused the absence of electrostatic repulsion/interaction between the synthesized CQDs, thus maintaining good nanoparticle stability. The formation of amine CQDs with neutral charges was possibly due to the protonation of amide groups formed during synthesis under moderately acidic conditions. Edward et al. reported that the use of a strong acid led to the protonation of amides with a predominantly formed O-protonate form. However, moderate acid conditions led to not only the protonation of amides with predominant O-protonate forms but also the appearance of N-protonate forms [30].

Next, FTIR analysis was conducted to investigate the molecular vibration features of the amine-modified CQDs (Fig. 3
(A)). The vibrational peaks detected at 3430 cm−1, 1715 cm−1, 1595 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrational absorptions of C–OH, −COO, −C═C− and C–O that are characteristic of CA, while the stretching vibration absorption bands detected at 3280 cm−1, 1580 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 (see Fig. S4(A–D)) belong to the vibration of the N–H group common to natural amines [31–33]. As displayed in Fig. 3(A), the stretching and bending vibrations detected at 2938 cm−1, 1656 cm−1, 1530 cm−1, and 1440 cm−1 belong to NH2, C═O, N–H, and C–N, and the presence of the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–NH–C at 1200 and 1130 cm−1 clearly demonstrated the existence of amide bonds on the surface of CCQDs, PCQDs, HCQDs and SCQDs. Moreover, the most significant absorption peak was observed at 1701 cm−1 and is attributed to the amide linkage (−CONH−), indicating that an amide group was formed on the surface of the prepared CQDs as a result of the interaction between the carboxyl group and amine group during microwave irradiation [24,33]. Furthermore, proof that an amide group was formed on the surface of the synthesized CQDs could be found in the disappearance of the stretching vibration peaks (C–OH, −COO, C–O and N–H) that were characteristic of the starting material used in the CQD synthesis. The disappearance of the C−OH, −COO, C-O and N–H functional groups was the result of thermal reduction during microwave synthesis and the formation of amide groups on the CQD surface [32]. Moreover, further evidence that amides were successfully involved in the surface modification of CQDs was obtained from measuring CQDs modified only with DETA (see Fig. S5). From the corresponding FTIR spectra, significant differences between the spectra could be clearly seen, indicating the presence of similarity in the formed CQDs. The most intense similarity between the amine-modified CQDs and “bare” CQDs was observed in the amide II region. The presence of the signal at1560 cm−1, which belongs to the CQDs, indicated that an amidic group (−R1−C(O)−NH−R2) was formed. Further differences in bands were observed at1047 cm−1, which were assigned to the C–O vibration. The change in the C–O vibrations of the CQD spectra further confirmed that amide groups were formed during the thermal decomposition process. As we mentioned before, these stretching vibration peaks disappeared under the thermal degradation of CA. However, it was obvious that the “bare” CQD spectra also did not show many vibrational peak characteristics of the characteristic for amine-modified CQDs mentioned in the text above. From the corresponding FTIR spectra, it could be clearly seen that significant changes were present in the case of amine-modified CQDs, thereby indicating successful surface modification.

The XPS survey spectrum of the amine-modified CQDs revealed the presence of three characteristic C1 s (carbon), N1 s (nitrogen) and O1 s (oxygen) peaks at 284.6 eV, 339.1 eV and 530.6 eV, respectively (see Fig. S6). These5 results suggested that the CCQD, PCQD, HCQD and SCQD samples contained the same chemical composition, where the atomic ratio among C:N:O was 68:14:17 (C/N/O). The high-resolution spectrum of C1 s showed the presence of three peaks at the same position in all samples (CCQDs, PCQDs, HCQDs and SCQDs), with binding energies of 287.1 eV, 285.1 eV and 283.7 eV (Fig. 3(B–E)). The peak detected at 283.7 eV corresponded to the aliphatic sp2 C (C–C), which might be related to the arrangement of carbon atoms in the conjugate lattice. The following peak detected at 285.1 eV was attributed to nitrous N-sp2 C (C–N), indicating the replacement of carbon atoms with nitrogen atoms inside/on the surface of CQDs. The last peak in the C1 s spectra detected at 287.1 eV belonged to sp2-bonded C with some oxygenated defects (C–O), clearly indicating the presence of amide groups on the CQD surface [34,35]. Similar to the C 1 s case, the N1 s spectrum of CCQDs, PCQDs, HCQDs and SCQDs contained two peaks at the same position with binding energies of 399.1 and 400.8 eV. The peak detected in the N1 s spectrum at 339.1 eV corresponded to “pyridinic” N (=N−), while the peak at 400.8 eV corresponded to “pyrrolic” N (−NH−). “Pyrrolic” N is usually located at the defect position or exposed edge of the plane, while “pyridinic” N is responsible for the replacement of C at the defect position [35,36]. Therefore, the presence of N atoms indicated the successful entry of N into the CQD structure and the replacement of carbon atoms with nitrogen atoms. Similar results could be observed after we analyzed the CQDs modified only with DETA (see Fig. S7). The most intensive peak occurred at285.0 eV, confirming the presence of the carbon chain (–C–C–) in the CQDs. Three peaks at similar intensities were assigned to –CH2–N– (285.4), –CH–N– (286.3 eV) and –CO(288.0 eV), representing the variety of bonds between nitrogen and carbon. In the N 1 s spectrum, only one peak at 399.8 eV was observed. This peak was assigned to nitrogen in the CQD system. The results collected during chemical composition analysis suggested no differences in the chemical structure between CQDs and CQDs modified with different amines. However, it was obvious that the chemical composition was changed if we looked at the percentage of groups formed on the surface of the amine-modified CQDs. In the bare CQDs, we could observe only 13% of the –CH–N– group, 12% of the –CO group and 60% of the –C–C– group. In contrast, in the amine-modified CQDs, the percentage of the –CH–N– group increased to nearly 50%, the –CO group increased to 20% and the –C–C– group decreased to 30%. These results fully corresponded with the FTIR and CQD AFM 3D scan profile, providing proof that the amine-modified CQDs were the result of a cross-linking process in which the carboxyl group of CA interacted with the amine group, thus resulting in the formation of amides arranged as C–N layers on the surface of the amine-modified CQDs.

The presence of carboxyl groups (CA) and amine groups (natural amines) in the solution after exposure to high temperature results in the polymerization and formation of CQDs. Polymerized CQDs under constant heating undergo dehydration and carbonization, thereby producing carbonized CQDs [37]. Xia et al. reported that molecules containing carboxyl, amino or hydroxyl groups were excellent precursors that could easily form condensates or undergo polymerization (polymer clusters) and further carbonization at moderate temperatures [38]. They revealed that at 150 °C, polymerized material increased the frequency of C–C/CC bonds (by 72%), confirming the carbonization of the formed polymers. Increasing the temperature up to 200 °C and 250 °C decreased the viscosity of the polymerized material and increased the frequency of carbonization (C–C/CC bonds) by 77% and 82%, respectively, proving that the polymer was [38] reduced [38]. Despite the fact that in our case, AFM, FTIR and XPS analysis clearly showed the formation of CQDs and the presence of amide groups on the CQD surface, the final confirmation of successive polymerization and carbonization of CQDs was verified by XRD and TGA analysis. The XRD patterns of CCQDs, PCQDs, HCQDs and SCQDs, as in previously conducted analyses, showed similar results with the presence of a broad diffraction peak with a high intensity centered at 2θ = 23° (Fig. 4 (C)). The diffraction peak attributed to the (002) lattice spacing belonged to highly disordered carbon with a graphitic nature. This result was in fair agreement with the results reported in other publications [39,40]. Hua et al. [39] described that the interlayer spacing of CQDs was broader than that for a graphite structure, indicating the presence of an amorphous structure. However, they found that in the CQD structure, abundant surface oxygen groups were present, which was characteristic of the graphite structure and confirmed the existence and crystalline nature of CQDs. Our results, obtained by FTIR and XPS, agreed with the results published by Hua et al. The FTIR spectrum showed characteristic peaks at 1550 cm−1, which were probably connected with the graphite band (G band) associated with the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a 2D-hexagonal lattice [41]. On the other hand, sp2-bonded C with oxygenated defects (C–O) was detected by XPS. This result clearly indicated the presence of amide groups and sp2 C (C–C), thus indicating the presence of lattice structures on the CQD surface.

The possible structural damage and thermal stability were further investigated by TGA analysis in a nitrogen atmosphere. Four types of quantum dots (CCQDs, HCQDs, PCQDs, and SCQDs) and six basic components (putrescine-PUT, spermine-SPE, histamine-HIS, cadaverine-CAD, diethylentriamine-DETA and citric acid-CA) were investigated. The decomposition curves of amine-modified CQDs heated in nitrogen at 10 °C/min to 500 °C are shown in Fig. 3(A), and the data are evaluated in Table 1 . The decomposition curves of the CQD basic components are shown in Fig. 4(B).

All four analyzed CQDs did not decompose completely while heating at 10 °C/min until 800 °C in N2, but 24–28% decomposed in air, and approximately 5% remained unable to be decomposed for all QDs. There was no difference in the number of decomposition steps (two) until 300 °C within one specimen regarding the dependence on heating rate, but decomposition temperatures were shifted to higher values at higher heating rates. It seemed that the compositions of the various CQDs were very similar, probably due to the basic material formed by the reaction of CA and DETA. Biogenic amines seemed to influence the decomposition of CQDs only slightly. The initial and relatively steep weight loss of CQDs until 100 °C was attributed to the release of low molecular components (probably water) from the basic material used in the CQD synthesis (DETA and CA). Usually, a slight loss of weight until 100 °C indicates good CQD structure stability [42]. The second decomposition step was attributed to the decomposition of other parts of the formed basic material, especially from CA (it reflected the second decomposition step of CA), and it was probably slightly influenced by the biogenic amines. The biogenic amines also influenced the main decomposition step. It might be that CAD decomposed during the first decomposition step, HIS during the second decomposition step and PUT and SPE during the main decomposition step. The degradation of covalent bonds associated with functional groups that belonged to the CQDs could be observed as a significant loss of weight at 400 °C [43]. Further weight loss at 500 °C indicated the release of CO2 and organic matter from the carbonized material, thereby leading to the conclusion that CQDs were successfully synthesized with excellent stability.

The surface functionalization of CQDs with amines directly affects the size, stability and surface charge of the nanoparticles, which can be directly observed through the increase or decrease in their photoluminescence (PL) properties. The UV−Vis spectra of the prepared CCQDs, PCQDs, HCQDs and CCQDs were examined to evaluate their photophysical behavior (Fig. 5 ). The synthesized samples showed strong fluorescence (blue color) properties under UV light at 312 nm. The absorption band at approximately 260–270 nm (Fig. 5(A)) corresponded to the biogenic amines alone; this band was not observed in the case of CQDs. The loss of that peak indicated that amines were efficiently conjugated to the CQD surface. Additionally, it was clear that the basic material used in the synthesis of CQDs did not show any fluorescence properties in comparison with the synthetized amine-modified CQDs. The absorption spectrum (Fig. 5(B)) of the amine-modified CQDs showed sharp high energy absorption centered at approximately 360 nm. Broad low energy absorption at approximately 340 nm was due to the π to π* transition of the sp2 domain (CC) in the CQD core and the n to π* transitions (CN bonds) on the CQD surface [33,44]. Moreover, the most attractive property of functionalized CQDs is their unique emissive behavior. Fig. 5(B) shows the PL emission of the CQDs under an excitation wavelength of 260 nm, showing a stable blue emission at λmax ≈ 460 nm. This might happen because the presence of different CQD functionalities may change the surrounding chemical and electronic environment. Additionally, the presence of an emission peak at the same wavelength could be attributed to the monodispersed nature of the CQDs in solution [45]. A study of the PL propertiesof CQDs was conducted at room temperature.

The PL properties of CQDs had an excitation wavelength-dependent feature, which could be changed under various environmental conditions. Usually, the changes in PL are reflected in the shifting of emission peaks that indicates changes to the size, charge or stability of CQDs. To study these changes, the synthesized CQDs were introduced to solutions with different pH values that mimic the expected environment in the human body. For that purpose, we used 1 mg of surface-coated or bare CQDs dispersed in 1 mL of the corresponding solution mimicking physiological conditions. The colloidal stability of surface-coated CQDs was studied upon dispersion in Ringeŕs solution, which is an isotonic solution mimicking the plasma environment, and second, in buffer mimicking the acidic environment of endosomes. Samples were stored for five weeks at room temperature under ambient light conditions. Although there were some changes in fluorescence intensity, they were not highly significant (Fig. 5(C)). The shifting of the emission peak was not observed. The prepared nanomaterial was found to be very stable in biological fluids, and a statistically significant change in fluorescence intensity was observed after five weeks in both tested solutions. Higher fluorescence intensities were observed when CQDs were introduced in acidic systems, and this difference might be attributed to the generation of residual charges within different environmental conditions [46]. At low pH (in acidic buffer), CQDs were predominantly protonated at the nitrogen site and bore a positive net charge, while in Ringer solution, which is slightly alkaline (pH7.4), only a small percentage of the carboxylic groups became deprotonated. This positive residual charge might lead to electrostatic interactions, which would diminish the fluorescence intensity and imply that the surface state of the CQDs is affected by the pH value. The observed high stability makes the synthesized CQDs promising candidates for biological purposes (antibacterial activity).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity studies of biogenic amine-modified CQDs
Study of CQDs in vitro cytotoxicity and their effect on cell structure
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility and cytotoxic effect of the prepared nanomaterial on HaCaT and HEK-293 cells were metabolically quantified through an MTT assay. The obtained results were plotted as the percentage of cell viability versus the concentration of CQDs and are shown in Fig. 6 (A). The obtained MTT results in the plots are related to the negative control (vehicle-treated cells – 100%) and expressed as viability percentage values. The CQD toxicity was studied in the concentration range of 0–500 µg/mL (lyophilized CQDs), and the obtained results were analyzed after 24 h. The obtained results did not reveal the presence of significant toxicity in either cell line. Compared with the control group cells, the treated HaCaT cells showed a slight decrease in viability with increasing concentrations of CQDs, and the cell viability was approximately 60% of the control when the concentrations of HCQDs, SCQDs and CCQDs reached 500 μg/mL. Only in the case of PCQDs, at each concentration, was there no significant cytotoxic effect. In contrast, after 24 h of posttreatment, HEK-293 cells showed excellent viability even up to the highest applied CQD concentration. These results clearly demonstrated that the prepared CQDs were practically nontoxic. The lack of any notable toxicity provides new opportunities for safe therapeutic application. A similar observation was reported by Jian et al. [47]. They tested the toxicity of spermine-coated CQDs in different concentration ranges (0–50 µg/mL) using the cellosaurus cell line. They found that at the highest treated concentration, the CQDs slightly stimulated the growth of cells. The same group investigated the cytotoxic effect of spermine/dopamine-coated CQDs [48]. They revealed that the viability of the cellosaurus cell line was 100% after treatment with the highest applied concentration of CQDs (100 µg/mL). Wu et al. reported that ethylenediamine-coated CQDs decreased Hela cell viability by 10% but only in a very high concentration range (0.5–1 mg/mL) [49]. Yang et al. coated CQDs with polyamine (tri(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine) and tested their the toxicity on a breast cell line [50]. The MTT assay results showed that CQDs at the highest concentration of 1 mg/mL did not induce a cytotoxic effect. Various studies have suggested that nanoparticles functionalized with carboxyl, amine or hydroxyl groups are easily internalized through the cell membrane by endocytosis due to the negative, positive or neutral nature of the mentioned groups and their interactions with surface proteins. However, the interaction of nanoparticles with the membrane surface can also increase their toxicity. Positively charged nanoparticles induce membrane fluidity and disturb membrane integrity, while negatively charged nanoparticles lead to membrane gelation and inhibit membrane function. Interestingly, nanoparticles without a charge show a decreased rate of cellular uptake and a decreased cytotoxic effect [51]. Nanoparticles modified by various functional groups can also cause lysosomal storage disease, which can be triggered by the accumulation of nanoparticles in cells. The functionalized nanoparticles induce the deficiency of lysosomal enzymes such as lysosomal sulfatases arylsulfatase A, B and G, thus causing lysosomal storage diseases and increasing toxicity in treated cell lines [52]. The MTT assay results clearly suggested that membrane disruption or lysosomal storage disease was not triggered after treatment of both cell lines with CQDs. Generally, this showed that the CQDs had excellent biocompatibility. Therefore, the above results led to the conclusion that the synthesized CQDs were not toxic when considering human cell lines.

To confirm our previous statement that these CQDs do not exhibit toxicity, we performed further experiments. Regarding the ability of CQDs to be easily transferred through the cell membrane, we observed the localization, integrity and disruption of the cell membrane under the microscope. The morphology of the cell lines was analyzed using a phase-contrast microscope 24 h after exposure to CQDs. In line with the MTT data, we did not observe any morphological features of apoptosis/necrosis due to amine-modified CQD exposure (Fig. 6(B)). Interestingly, the confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) micrographs clearly indicate the presence of well-distributed CQDs in the cytoplasm, presumably due to their small size that results in their facile intracellular uptake, particularly in epithelial HEK-293 cells (Fig. 7 ). The CLSM micrographs also confirmed that no signs of apoptosis or necrosis, such as apoptotic bodies or cell shrinkage, were present and that the CQDs did not affect the reorganization of the F-actin cytoskeleton. The beginning of the intracellular transport of nanoparticles started with their internalization into the cells via endosomes (membrane-bound intracellular vesicles) [53]. Multiple studies connected to nanoparticle transport described the transfer of nanoparticles by endosome vesicles to cellular destinations [54]. These studies also described that some nanoparticles trapped in vesicles were usually recycled by hydrolytic enzymes after the endosome vesicle integrated with the lysosomes. However, other nanoparticles usually escaped by being released into the cytoplasm and bypassing lysosomal degradation [55]. The escaped nanoparticles were usually exposed to intercellular degradation by a process called autophagy in which the nanoparticles were surrounded by autophagosomes and delivered to the lysosome for final degradation [56,57].

Generally, nanoparticles in biological fluid environments have a strong tendency to form a layer of protein on their surface (corona protein) [58]. To maintain cellular homeostasis, autophagy degrades aggregated proteins and dysfunctional organelles. It has also been reported that autophagy is involved in nonmetabolic functions, for instance, in blood cells [59]. Therefore, we investigated the formation of the protein corona on the surface of the amine-functionalized CQDs, which could negatively influence the behavior and application of CQDs (see Fig. S8). Some small amounts of proteins were found on the surface of all tested CQDs (mainly albumin ~66 kDa). However, the most abundant protein (albumin) was found in the control plasma sample and plasma sample treated with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at a ratio of 1:1. Therefore, the formation of a protein corona was nonsignificant after the CQD application, suggesting that the synthesized CQDs could potentially be used in the future as a new type of drug with no toxicity or effect on cellular stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amine-modified CQD effects on the formation of ROS and on DNA fragmentation
ce:section.ce:para: Further analysis of amine-modified CQD toxicity was conducted by fluorescence microscopy to study the formation of ROS and of DNA intercalation. The formation of ROS within cells exposed to QDs can be considered a major contributor to their toxicological effects that lead to damage in cellular functions. To determine the production of ROS, two different cell lines were ROS/nuclei stained and treated with the synthetized CQDs. No evidence of ROS production was observed in the HaCaT and HEK-293 cells after CQD exposure. Additionally, nuclear staining was visualized using DAPI fluorescence, which binds strongly to DNA. DAPI nuclear counterstaining revealed no evidence of chromatin condensation, removing the possibility of CQD effects on DNA in both treated cell lines (Fig. 8 (A and B)). To confirm that the toxic effect of CQDs did not exist, we performed control experiments (see Fig. S9). The formation of ROS and condensation of chromatin in HEK-293 and HaCaT cells were examined by PBS at pH 7.4 as a negative control without H2O2 and with 100% H2O2 activity as a positive control. The obtained results indicated that the treatment of human cell lines with the highest concentration (500 µg/mL) of CQDs did not induce the condensation of chromatin and formation of ROS compared with the 100% H2O2 activity % of the positive control. The absence of chromatin condensation and disruption of the DNA strand was also confirmed by a comet assay.

The comet assay analyzed the effects of the carcinogens on the single- or double-strand breaks in DNA. Observing the comet tail intensity, we were able to calculate the total amount of DNA breaks in the cells. The comet assay was performed only at the highest CQD concentration (500 µg/mL), and the results were observed after 24 h (Fig. 9 (A)). The presence of genotoxicity was not detected after 24 h in the case of all applied amine-modified CQDs. As shown in Fig. 9(A), the DNA comet tails in the HaCaT and HEK-293 cells exposed to CQDs were comparable with that of the negative control. No significant DNA damage was observed in cells treated with CQDs at the highest concentration of 500 μg/mL. DNA damage was only detected after treatment with the positive control (H2O2), which is known as an agent that induces strand breakage and the loss of the supercoiled structure. Collecting the results from 50 randomly selected comets, we found that after 24 h, all DNA damage induced by CQDs was repaired quickly by the appropriate cell repair proteins (Fig. 9(B)) in both cell lines (HaCaT and HEK-293). The amine-modified CQD damage level expressed in comet grade was found to be small (grades 1 and 2) and probably induced by the effect of stress after treatment. After 24 h of treatment, only H2O2 damage (grade 4) was observed. In published works, it is well described that CQDs have a strong ability to generate ROS due to the large surface area of their highly reactive surface. As a foreign material, CQDs have massive oxidizing ability, and the production of ROS can be taken as a standard effect [60]. Depending on the surface modification or environmental conditions, CQDs can also demonstrate dual properties and behave as pro-oxidants or as antioxidants [61]. However, we assume that the absence of ROS and condensation of chromatin in both treated cell lines was most likely due to the activation of the previously mentioned autophagy process and the degradation of CQDs by lysosomes. Taken together, the synthetized CQDs clearly exhibited excellent compatibility with eukaryotic organisms.

Multidrug resistance among microorganisms threatens the clinical or public prevention of bacterial infections. Therefore, alternative antimicrobial strategies are being developed, including the use of nanotechnology as a tool to combat the development of multidrug resistance among bacterial species. To date, different studies dealing with nanoparticle antibacterial activity and the development of resistance have been published [62,63]. To find an alternative to antibiotics, we tested an in-house prepared nanomaterial (CCQDs, PCQDs, HCQDs, and SCQDs) as an antibacterial agent. Liu et al., observed the synergistic effect of nanoparticles and biogenic polyamine spermine against multidrug-resistant S. aureus [64] . Therefore, we assumed that modifying the surface of CQDs with biogenic amines should lead to an improvement in the antimicrobial effect of CQDs. Before antimicrobial testing, the concentration of biogenic amines on the surface of CQDs was determined using elemental analysis. The elemental analysis results are presented in Table 2 . The surface coverage of amino ligands (RNH2), which corresponded to natural amines, could be calculated from the difference in nitrogen content between the unmodified and modified biogenic amine CQDs. The amount of nitrogen that belonged to the biogenic amines was determined by the difference in the nitrogen amount of the respective quantum dots and the amount of nitrogen in the control sample (DETA). The concentration of amines was calculated according to the following equation:
Mass

n a t u r a l

m a s s

W N

The equation calculates the concentration of active sites, in mol per gram, through the use of the molar mass of nitrogen, where Nc is the amount of nitrogen in unmodified CQDs, Na is the amount of nitrogen in the respective amine-modified CQDs and AWN is the atomic mass of nitrogen. The concentration of biogenic amines within the carbon core was calculated to be 38.7 µg for cadaverine, 27.66 µg for histamine, 103.99 µg for spermine tetrachloride and 54.14 µg for putrescine.

The antimicrobial effect of CQDs was tested on different gram-positive (S. aureus) and gram-negative (E. coli, K. pneumoniae) bacterial strains. The results from the growth curve method were calculated as a percentage of inhibition (Fig. 10 (A–D)). The concentrations of the respective amines were in different ranges for individual CQDs. All CQD formulations caused 100% bacterial inhibition in the case of S. aureus. The total growth reduction in gram-negative bacteria was achieved by CCQD and HCQD treatment. However, the E. coli growth was only reduced to 74% by SCQDs and to 50% by PCQDs at the highest applied concentration. The CCQDs and HCQDs showed a 100% decrease in E. coli viability at low concentrations (4–8 μg/mL). K. pneumoniae viability was reduced to 19–30% in a low concentration range (4–8 μg/mL) by SCQDs and from 10 to 36% by PCQDs in a concentration range of 4–8 μg/mL. However, in the low concentration range, the CCQDs and HCQDs showed a decrease in K. pneumoniae viability of up to 80%. We also noticed that at the highest concentration, all synthetized CQDs decreased the viability of the three tested bacteria with results between 80 and 100%. The comparison of MICs is shown in Table 3 . The PCQDs demonstrated the lowest MIC value (3.4 µg/mL) for S. aureus. Regarding E. coli and K. pneumoniae, the lowest MIC values were with HCQDs (6.9 and 13.8 µg/mL, respectively), which was in contrast with that of gram-positive bacteria, where the HCQD MIC was the highest.

The antibacterial activity of “bare” CQDs was also tested by the growth curve method for 24 h in a wide concentration range (0, 8, 16 and 24 µg/mL). To exclude any doubts about “bare” CQD antibacterial activity, the CQDs were tested on the same bacterial species (see Fig. S10(A)). As expected, “bare” CQDs did not have a significant antibacterial effect. Through analysis of the growth curve, we found that CQDs in some cases significantly increased the viability of bacterial strains after 24 h of exposure. Additionally, the antibacterial effect of amines was a few orders of magnitude lower than that of the amine-modified CQDs. The highest activity was found for putrescine, where the MIC was 250 µg/mL. For the other amines, the MIC was not observed for tested concentrations up to 500 µg/mL. At a concentration of 500 µg/mL, spermidine caused at most 38%, histamine 12%, and cadaverine 10% inhibition of bacterial growth (see. Fig. S10(B–E)). Bing and colleagues tested three types of CQDs with different surface charges. The positively charged CQDs modified with spermine exhibited a strong antibacterial effect, while the negatively or uncharged CQDs had almost no activity. They reported that the effective antimicrobial concentration (IC50) was 300 µg/mL. The positively charged spermine-coated CQDs were attracted to the negative charge of the bacterial (E. coli) membrane. In a sufficient concentration, the membrane surface of E. coli was covered, resulting in the inhibition of normal membrane function. Such conditions triggered the accumulation of ROS and DNA/RNA damage [15]. On the other hand, Jian et al. reported the antimicrobial activity of spermidine-coated CQDs with an MIC of 2 µg/mL. The CQDs were effective against gramG-positive and gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and S. enteritidis). However, they also reported that the main antimicrobial mechanism of CQDs was directly connected with the positive charge of CQDs and the disruption of the bacterial membrane. Interestingly, no formation of ROS species has been reported, confirming the disordered influx/efflux of ions across the bacterial membrane [65]. Li et al. also reported the antibacterial activity of spermidine-coated CQDs. They found that MIC values for gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus and B. subtilis), two non-MDR gram-negative bacteria (E. coli and P. aeruginosa), and one MDR gram-positive (MRSA) bacterium were is 0.9 µg/mL. Our nanoparticle functionalization exhibited a neutral charge, which could cause a difference in the antibacterial effect. Moreover, gram-negative bacteria have a sophisticated low permeable envelope barrier system, with lipopolysaccharides in the outer membrane and efflux pumps; this sophisticated system is able to exclude drugs and other xenobiotics from bacterial cells [66,67].

The antibacterial effect was also evaluated by the visualization of living and dead bacteria through the use of a live/dead assay and fluorescence microscopy. The green fluorescent stain SYTO9 is able to permeate all cells (living and dead), whereas the red fluorescent stain propidium iodide (PI) is membrane impermeant and enters only damaged cells; both of these compounds stain nucleic acids [68]. The fluorescence images of S. aureus treated with SCQDs are shown in Fig. 10(E). The results are in accordance with the MIC values. When two concentrated MIC values (13 µg/mL) were applied, almost no bacteria (green spots) were observed. While using the MIC value (6.5 µg/mL), the presence of S. aureus was obvious (green spots); however, the abundance of red spots indicated that the membranes were damaged, thereby leading to bacterial cell death due to the loss of membrane integrity by SCQD treatment. The lowest concentration (3.25 µg/mL, half of MIC) inhibited S. aureus growth compared with that of the control; nevertheless, the occurrence of damaged cells (red spots) was distinctly lower than with the MIC value. A similar trend was also observed for S. aureus after treatment with PCQDs (Fig. 10(F)).

To identify ROS production inside the bacterial cells, CellROX green reagent was employed. Fig. 11 (A) shows ROS formation in S. aureus cultures treated with different concentrations of PCQDs. There was no observable bacterial growth when using a concentration of 6.8 µg/mL (twice the MIC). With the treatment of 3.4 µg/mL, there was an increase in green fluorescence spots (ROS signal) compared with the control. PCQDs at a concentration of 1.7 µg/mL, also showed increased ROS formation compared with the control. The number of bacterial cells (blue spots) was consistent with the live/dead assay results (Fig. 10(F)). Considering these results, it could be concluded that CQD treatment caused ROS induction in bacterial cells, which was apparently associated with the determined antibacterial effect. Oxidative stress in bacterial cells after exposure to ROS harms cellular components, such as DNA, membrane lipids and proteins [69]. Therefore, oxidative stress is considered to be a key antibacterial mechanism of nanomaterials [70,71], which includes amine-modified CQDs [72].

The surface structures of the untreated (control) and treated (SCQDs and PCQDs) S. aureus cells visualized by cryo-SEM are shown in Fig. 11(B and C). The untreated S. aureus cells showed a smooth spherical morphology with intact cytoplasmic membranes. After SCQD treatment, the shape of S. aureus cells shriveled, and the surface became crimped (Fig. 11 (B)). The distorted morphology was most evident at high SCQD concentrations (26 and 13 µg/mL); however, morphological changes were observable even at low concentrations. The PCQD treatment caused morphological alterations as well (Fig. 11(C)). The cells became shriveled and blurry at high PCQD concentrations (27 and 20.3 µg/mL). Due to containing multiple cationic charges, amine-coated CQDs could easily interact with bacterial membranes. Jian et al. reported that positively charged spermine-coated CQDs interacted through electrostatic interactions with negatively charged bacterial membranes, which resulted in a loss of bacterial membrane integrity and an uncontrolled influx/efflux of ions across the membrane. However, it was interesting that they did not observe the formation of ROS [19]. The same phenomenon induced by the binding of SCQDs to membrane proteins, porins or peptidoglycans via electrostatic interactions or multivalent hydrogen bonding, thus causing cell envelope disruption and bacterial morphology alteration, was described by Li et al. [9]. However, our results were contradictory with the results reported by other authors because of the neutral charge of our CQDs. It is well known that spermine and putrescine have important roles in bacterial cells. They are responsible for various processes tightly regulated by the influx/efflux mechanisms and cellular metabolism. These include the regulation of gene transcription and translation and the, regulation of membrane permeability (ion channels), along with providing protection from oxidative stress, toxin activity, and bacteriocin production. On the other hand, both polyamines are directly included in the stability of membrane surfaces through the protection of functional and structural stabilities of porin and polyamine transport [65]. Although a large number of studies report the disintegration of membranes due to charge-driven interactions between the bacterial and antimicrobial compounds, very few reports signify the importance of disrupting membrane permeability.

In prokaryotic cells, polyamines have been reported as modulators of membrane permeability governed by specific interactions with transport proteins, porins [73,74]. Porins mediate the passive diffusion of hydrophilic compounds across the bacterial membrane, and their activity is quickly affected by ligand binding. It is generally considered that after binding with porins, polyamines inhibit porin channel opening, thereby promoting its closed state in E. coli [75]. However, they have also been described as permeabilizers of the outer membranes of gram-negative bacteria in the study of Yasuda et al. They monitored changes in membrane potential by following the distribution of tetraphenylphosphonium and calcium ions and confirmed membrane permeabilization through the release of lipopolysaccharide molecules [76]. Our results suggest that SCQDs and PCQDs created bacterial membrane damage that was visible in the SEM images. Such events were not visible through the use of HCQDs and CCQDs. This led us to the logical explanation that SCQDs and PCQDs interact with porin proteins on the bacterial membrane surface and disturbs polyamine transport. This mechanism probably induced the strong interaction and destabilization of the cell membrane function, as well as inhibited membrane synthesis. Furthermore, such destabilization of the membrane could potentially trigger an uncontrolled influx/efflux of ions across the membrane. At this point, it was not clear whether biogenic amine CQDs increased or decreased the level of efflux, but after direct binding to porin channels, they unquestionably had an effect on membrane permeabilization. These findings correlated with our results obtained by cryo-SEM. Cryo-SEM revealed that after CQD treatment, the membrane surface was covered with the visible formation of a crystal structure, which was probably because of ion aggregation.

However, our previous research strongly suggested that CQDs had strong tendencies to interact with bacterial DNA [77]. Therefore, we also believed that the growth of bacteria was terminated by affecting their gene replication, transcription, and translation mechanisms through interactions with the bacterial DNA or RNA. Our findings suggest that CQDs also induced bacterial cell death by increasing the release of ROS, which was probably related to the direct intercalation into DNA and the inability of the bacteria to repair the same DNA. The inability to repair DNA was directly connected with the already destabilized membrane function. This conclusion was because ROS species were not detected in our healthy human cell lines. We assumed that one of the possible reasons was that human DNA could be repaired much faster than bacterial DNA. We also believe that because of the small size of CQDs, their interaction with the human cell membrane was bypassed, and the membrane function was undisturbed; in contrast, in bacteria cells this was not the case with bacterial cells. The comet results clearly indicated the presence of DNA damage at the starting point of treatment. However, after 24 h of initial damage, the DNA was 100% repaired. Additionally, as we mentioned in the manuscript, the absence of ROS and the condensation of chromatin in both treated human cell lines were most likely due to the activation of the previously mentioned autophagy process and degradation of CQDs by lysosomes. Therefore, we believe that compared with bacterial cells, that human cells had more sophisticated mechanisms for discharging foreign materials. Similar findings were also reported by several authors. Bing et al. reported that spermine-coated CQDs had the ability to bind to the negatively charged membrane surfaces, resulting in the inhibition of membrane surface function and increasing ROS activity, which directly damaged DNA and triggered apoptosis [78]. Huang et al. described that 2,2-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine)-coated CQDs simply adhered to the bacterial membrane surface, and by diffusion, they penetrated the cell membranes and induced oxidative stress. They reported that covering the membrane surface led to indirect toxicity because nutrients could not be transported inside and toxins could not be released. This resulted in the accumulation of ROS, which damaged DNA/RNA and inhibited gene expression [79]. In both cases, the author reported the absence of toxicity and the formation of ROS in human cell lines.

In summary, by a simple thermal decomposition route, small, uncharged CQDs were fabricated, while amines were used for surface modification. A systematic study on CQD-induced cytotoxicity in two healthy HEK-293 and HaCaT human cell lines was performed, revealing the high biocompatibility of the synthesized CQDs. Based on our findings, all four types of CQDs invoked similar responses, probably due to their similar characteristics, such as their neutral charge and surface functional groups. The intracellular localization of all CQDs by HEK-293 and HaCaT cells was ascertained by CLSM. In vitro cytotoxicity tests showed a negligible decrease in cell viability in comparison with untreated cells. Moreover, characteristic apoptotic hallmarks were not observed. Additionally, we studied the effect of the protein corona at the interface between the CQDs and biological components and considered the effect the protein corona could have on the CQD properties and their desired activity [80]. The observed low binding with plasma proteins was attributed to the neutral surface charge of the prepared CQDs. Similar behavior has already been described, and control over the protein corona has been reported previously to render nanoparticles more biocompatible and to allow them to function better [81,82]. The amine-modified CQDs did not contain a metal core, which improved the quality of the nanoparticles, including their undesired toxicity in eukaryotic cell lines. The synthesized CQDs were evaluated for their bactericidal activity, and in vitro experiments were conducted with S. aureus, E. coli and K. pneumoniae. Propidium iodide staining of CQD-treated bacteria provided evidence of damage to the bacterial membrane, which was further examined by SEM. The SEM images showed distortion in the cell morphology and changes in the membrane integrity were caused by direct contact between the CQDs and bacteria. The greater effectiveness of the CQDs toward gram-positive S. aureus was probably due to the lack of a low permeable envelope barrier system (lipopolysaccharides) present in the membrane of gram-negative bacteria.

The neutral charge of our QDs excluded the possibility of their ionic interaction with the charged components of the bacterial membrane. Therefore, intrinsically generated ROS seemed to be a bacterial apoptosis-inducing trigger since elevated ROS production was observed in S. aureus. While CQDs with antibacterial properties have been reported so far, neutral charge-driven activity has not yet been described. The fundamental criteria that directed the synthesis of soluble and biocompatible CQDs were (1) easy functionalization, (2) stability over a large pH range, (3) low nonspecific adsorption, and (4) small size essential for accessing confined biological compartments. Using CA and biogenic amines, beneficial CQDs that met all these criteria were prepared. A key advantage of the synthesized CQDs was their zero net charge; their slight positive charge was linked to the nonuniform distribution of radial charge [83]. The neutral charge of the prepared CQDs resulted in high colloidal stability regardless of the pH along with nontoxic and noninteracting behavior. To address the problem of colloidal stability, many strategies for generating macroscopically neutral QDs have been described. Xu et al., reported a cysteine-rich peptide coating for generating small, biocompatible QDs with improved properties [84]. Very recently, lipoic acid derivatives have been reported as surfactants for yielding nonadhesive, biocompatible QDs for in vivo imaging [85]. Sulfobetaine, a tripeptide glutathione, and penicillamine were also used as ligands to provide QDs with benefits for in vivo applications [86–88]. In general, the above studies highlighted the importance of surface charge on the desorption and adsorption of proteins, which later might trigger unwanted biological effects. A study by Bing showed that the antibacterial effect of spermine-modified CQDs, when uncharged, was absent. The activity of CQDs was attributed exclusively to the positive surface charge-triggered interaction with the bacterial membrane. According to our observations, SCQDs influenced membrane integrity. This result suggested that the functional groups on the CQDs influenced the antibacterial properties more than the charge itself. It has been demonstrated that amines have an important role in modulating the outer membrane permeability of bacteria [89]. The bactericidal activity of polyamine analogs via permeabilization was evidenced and reported by Balakrishna et al. [90]. Therefore, by utilizing the same mechanism, we assumed that the surface molecules employed in this study mediated the transfer of CQDs inside the bacterial cells. Once inside, the generation of ROS was induced and triggered apoptosis due to irreparable DNA damage. Although the current information did not explain the correct mechanism of CQDs, their antibacterial activity in terms of DNA damage or in the downregulation of gene expression have yet to be elucidated. Nevertheless, the findings of this work may reduce ongoing debate concerning the toxic effects of QDs and facilitate their biomedical implementation. The exceptionally high stability and absence of cytotoxicity of amine-modified CQDs to human HaCaT keratinocytes render them applicable for wound healing or treating skin infections treating. This study provides useful information on cell cytotoxicity, although the molecular mechanisms need further investigation. Thus, our findings may open a way for the design of novel antibacterial nanoparticles.
